title: 92.	DP on trees: Rerooting DP (sum of distances in tree).
description: 92.	DP on trees: Rerooting DP (sum of distances in tree).

examples.explanation: Tree: 0-1-2 and 1-3. For node 0: DFS: start sum=0; visit 0 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; from 1: neighbor 2 (d=2) sum=3; neighbor 3 (d=2) sum=5; answer[0]=5. Node 1: DFS: start sum=0; visit 1 (d=0) sum=0; neighbors: 0 (d=1) sum=1; 2 (d=1) sum=2; 3 (d=1) sum=3; answer[1]=3. Node 2: DFS: start sum=0; visit 2 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; from 1: neighbors 0 (d=2) sum=3; 3 (d=2) sum=5; answer[2]=5. Node 3: similar to node 2: sum=5; answer[3]=5. Final: [5,3,5,5].

Tree: 0 connected to 1 and 2. Node 0: DFS: start sum=0; visit 0 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; neighbor 2 (d=1) sum=2; answer[0]=2. Node 1: DFS: start sum=0; visit 1 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 0 (d=1) sum=1; from 0: neighbor 2 (d=2) sum=3; answer[1]=3. Node 2: similar: sum=3; answer[2]=3. Final: [2,3,3].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) DFS
approaches.summary: For each node, perform a DFS to compute the sum of distances to all other nodes by traversing the entire tree. This is straightforward but inefficient for large trees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Sum of Distances in Tree
ladders.desc: Compute sum of distances from each node to all other nodes using O(n²) DFS for each node.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the 'Sum of Distances in Tree' problem. The intuition is straightforward: for each node in the tree, we need to calculate the total distance to every other node. Since the tree has n nodes, we can perform a DFS starting from each node, accumulating distances as we traverse to all other nodes. The brute force approach works because a tree is a connected acyclic graph, so DFS from any node will reach all other nodes exactly once along unique paths. We maintain a visited set to avoid cycles and recursively compute distances. For each node u, we initialize sum=0, then DFS to neighbor v with current distance d+1, adding to sum. After DFS from all nodes, we have the answer array. Time complexity is O(n²) because we run DFS (O(n)) for each of n nodes. Space is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L1 as L0 implements the full algorithm using the DFS subroutine. Common mistakes include not resetting visited set between DFS calls (causing incorrect distances) or missing the base case in recursion. Real code pattern: def sumOfDistances(n, edges): answer = [0]*n; for i in range(n): visited = set(); def dfs(u, d): visited.add(u); nonlocal sum; sum += d; for v in adj[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v, d+1); sum = 0; dfs(i, 0); answer[i] = sum; return answer.

examples.trace: Tree: 0-1-2 and 1-3. For node 0: DFS: start sum=0; visit 0 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; from 1: neighbor 2 (d=2) sum=3; neighbor 3 (d=2) sum=5; answer[0]=5. Node 1: DFS: start sum=0; visit 1 (d=0) sum=0; neighbors: 0 (d=1) sum=1; 2 (d=1) sum=2; 3 (d=1) sum=3; answer[1]=3. Node 2: DFS: start sum=0; visit 2 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; from 1: neighbors 0 (d=2) sum=3; 3 (d=2) sum=5; answer[2]=5. Node 3: similar to node 2: sum=5; answer[3]=5. Final: [5,3,5,5].

Tree: 0 connected to 1 and 2. Node 0: DFS: start sum=0; visit 0 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1 (d=1) sum=1; neighbor 2 (d=1) sum=2; answer[0]=2. Node 1: DFS: start sum=0; visit 1 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 0 (d=1) sum=1; from 0: neighbor 2 (d=2) sum=3; answer[1]=3. Node 2: similar: sum=3; answer[2]=3. Final: [2,3,3].

testCases.expected: Invalid (not a tree, but if treated as tree with n=3 edges ignored, assume it's acyclic input)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS from a Single Root
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from a given root node to compute sum of distances to all other nodes.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach: given a tree and a starting root node, compute the total distance from that root to every other node using DFS. The intuition is that in a tree, the path from root to any node is unique, so DFS can traverse all nodes, accumulating distances as we go deeper. We start at the root with distance 0, visit it, then recursively visit each unvisited neighbor with distance increased by 1, adding that distance to a running sum. The algorithm: initialize sum=0 and visited set; define dfs(u, d): add u to visited, sum += d; for each neighbor v of u: if v not visited, call dfs(v, d+1); start with dfs(root, 0). Time complexity is O(n) for one DFS as we visit each node once. Space is O(n) for recursion stack and visited set. This feeds into L0 by being called for each node. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass visited set or resetting it incorrectly, or not handling base case. Real code pattern: def dfs_sum(root, adj): visited = set(); total = 0; def dfs(u, d): visited.add(u); nonlocal total; total += d; for v in adj[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v, d+1); dfs(root, 0); return total.

examples.trace: Tree: 0-1-2, 1-3. Start at root 0, sum=0. DFS: visit 0 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 1: dfs(1,1): visit 1 sum=1; neighbors: 2: dfs(2,2): visit 2 sum=3; 3: dfs(3,2): visit 3 sum=5. Return sum=5.
Tree: 0-1 and 0-2. Start at root 1, sum=0. DFS: visit 1 (d=0) sum=0; neighbor 0: dfs(0,1): visit 0 sum=1; neighbors: 2: dfs(2,2): visit 2 sum=3. Return sum=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Accumulating Distances During Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: The technique of summing distances while traversing a tree via DFS/BFS.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the idea that during a tree traversal (DFS or BFS), we can compute the sum of distances from a starting node by maintaining a running total that increments with the depth of each visited node. The intuition is that distance in a tree is simply the number of edges traversed from the start to each node, so as we explore, we add the current depth to a sum. In DFS, we start at the root with depth 0, add 0 for the root, then for each child, we recurse with depth+1 and add that depth. This works because each node is visited exactly once, and its depth corresponds to its distance from the root. The algorithm: initialize sum=0; define traverse(node, depth): sum += depth; for each child of node: traverse(child, depth+1). Time O(n), space O(n) for recursion. This is the heart of L1's DFS subroutine. Common mistakes: incorrect depth increment or missing the root's contribution. Real code pattern: total = 0; def accumulate(u, d): total += d; for v in children[u]: accumulate(v, d+1); accumulate(root, 0).

examples.input: root=0, tree: 0->1, 0->2 (directed for simplicity), depths from root
examples.trace: Start total=0. accumulate(0,0): add depth 0, total=0; children: accumulate(1,1): add 1, total=1; accumulate(2,1): add 1, total=2. Return 2.

Start total=0. accumulate(1,0): add 0, total=0; children: accumulate(0,1): add 1, total=1; accumulate(2,1): add 1, total=2; from 2: accumulate(3,2): add 2, total=4? Wait, correct: accumulate(1,0): total=0; accumulate(0,1): total=1; accumulate(2,1): total=2; from 2: accumulate(3,2): total=4. Actually, distances: 1 to 0=1, 1 to 2=1, 1 to 3=2, sum=4. But output should be 4, not 6. Let's fix: tree edges: [[1,0],[1,2],[2,3]]. From root 1: distances: to 0=1, to 2=1, to 3=2, sum=4. Trace: accumulate(1,0): total=0; go to 0: accumulate(0,1): total=1; back to 1, go to 2: accumulate(2,1): total=2; from 2 to 3: accumulate(3,2): total=4. Output 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a tree using DFS while keeping track of current depth from start.
ladders.explanation: This building block is about performing a depth-first search (DFS) on a tree and maintaining the depth (distance from starting node) as we recurse. The intuition is that in tree traversal, we move from node to node via edges, and each edge increases depth by 1. This is fundamental for computing distances. We use recursion: at each node, we note its depth, then recursively visit children with depth+1. The algorithm: define dfs(node, depth): process node (e.g., print or store depth); for each neighbor of node not visited: mark visited, dfs(neighbor, depth+1). Time O(n), space O(n). This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where L2 adds summation. Common mistakes: incorrect depth initialization or not passing depth in recursion. Real code pattern: def dfs(u, d, visited, adj): visited.add(u); print(f'Node {u} at depth {d}'); for v in adj[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v, d+1, visited, adj).

examples.input: start=0, tree: 0-1, 0-2, traverse and output depths
examples.trace: Start dfs(0,0): visit 0, depth 0; neighbors: 1 not visited, dfs(1,1): visit 1, depth 1; back to 0, neighbor 2 not visited, dfs(2,1): visit 2, depth 1.

examples.input: start=1, tree: 0-1, 1-2, traverse and output depths
examples.trace: Start dfs(1,0): visit 1, depth 0; neighbors: 0 not visited, dfs(0,1): visit 0, depth 1; back to 1, neighbor 2 not visited, dfs(2,1): visit 2, depth 1.

testCases.expected: Node 0 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 2 depth 1
Node 0 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 2 depth 2
Node 1 depth 0, Node 0 depth 1, Node 2 depth 1
Node 0 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 3 depth 2, Node 2 depth 1
Node 2 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 0 depth 2
Node 0 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 2 depth 2, Node 3 depth 3
Node 1 depth 0, Node 0 depth 1, Node 2 depth 1, Node 3 depth 2
Node 0 depth 0, Node 1 depth 1, Node 3 depth 2, Node 4 depth 2, Node 2 depth 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS on a Tree
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search to visit all nodes in a tree.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is about traversing all nodes in a tree using DFS. A tree is a connected acyclic graph, so DFS ensures we visit each node exactly once by recursively exploring neighbors and avoiding revisits. The intuition: start at any node, mark it visited, then for each adjacent node not visited, recurse. This works because trees have no cycles, so we won't get stuck. The algorithm: initialize visited set; define dfs(node): visited.add(node); for each neighbor of node: if neighbor not visited: dfs(neighbor). Time O(n) where n is nodes, space O(n) for recursion stack. This is foundational for L3, which adds depth tracking. Common mistakes: not marking node as visited before recursion (causing infinite loops) or missing base case. Real code pattern: def dfs(u, visited, adj): visited.add(u); for v in adj[u]: if v not in visited: dfs(v, visited, adj).

examples.output: Visited: 0,1,2 or 0,2,1 (order depends on neighbor list)
examples.trace: Start dfs(0): visit 0; neighbors: assume order [1,2]; dfs(1): visit 1; neighbors of 1: none, return; dfs(2): visit 2; done. Order: 0,1,2.
Start dfs(1): visit 1; neighbors: assume [0,2]; dfs(0): visit 0; neighbors of 0: done; dfs(2): visit 2; done. Order: 1,0,2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree as a Graph
ladders.desc: Understand a tree as a connected acyclic graph with nodes and edges.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces trees as a special type of graph. A tree is an undirected graph that is connected (there is a path between any two nodes) and acyclic (has no cycles). It has n nodes and n-1 edges. The intuition: trees are hierarchical structures where each node (except root in rooted trees) has a parent, but in this problem, we treat it as an unrooted graph. This is crucial because DFS on a tree will visit all nodes without repetition due to no cycles. Key properties: unique path between any two nodes, which makes distance computation straightforward. We represent trees using adjacency lists: for each node, a list of neighbors. This feeds into L4 as DFS operates on this structure. Common mistakes: confusing trees with general graphs (trees have exactly n-1 edges for n nodes) or not handling connectivity. Real code pattern: n = number of nodes; adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edges: connect 0 and 1, connect 1 and 2. Check: connected? Yes, path 0-1-2. Acyclic? Yes, no cycles. Number edges=2, nodes=3, satisfies n-1=2.
Nodes: 0,1,2,3. Edges: 0-1, 0-2, 0-3. Connected? Yes, all connect to 0. Acyclic? Yes. Edges=3, nodes=4, n-1=3.

testCases.expected: Not a tree (cycle), but assume valid input for practice

approaches.name: DP with Rerooting O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two DFS passes: first compute subtree sizes and distances from a root, then reroot to update distances for all nodes using DP formulas. This efficiently leverages tree structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Sum of Distances for All Nodes in a Tree
ladders.desc: Given an undirected tree with n nodes, compute for each node the sum of distances to all other nodes using rerooting DP in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires computing for each node the sum of distances to every other node in an undirected tree. The naive approach would be O(n²) by running BFS/DFS from each node. The optimal O(n) solution uses rerooting dynamic programming with two DFS passes. The intuition is that when we move the root from a parent to its child, we can update the distance sum using a formula that leverages previously computed values. First DFS (post-order): Compute subtree sizes and initial distance sum from root 0. For each node, subtreeSize[node] = 1 + sum of subtree sizes of children. distSum[0] = sum of subtree sizes of all other nodes (since each edge contributes its subtree size to the total distance). Second DFS (pre-order): Reroot from parent to child. When moving root from u to v (where v is child of u), the distance sum changes because: 1) All nodes in v's subtree get 1 unit closer (decrease by subtreeSize[v]), 2) All nodes outside v's subtree get 1 unit farther (increase by n - subtreeSize[v]). So distSum[v] = distSum[u] - subtreeSize[v] + (n - subtreeSize[v]) = distSum[u] + n - 2*subtreeSize[v]. This formula allows us to compute all distance sums in O(n) time after the initial DFS. The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from edges. 2) First DFS from root 0: compute subtree sizes and distSum[0]. 3) Second DFS from root 0: apply rerooting formula to compute distSum for all children. Time: O(n) for two DFS traversals. Space: O(n) for adjacency list, subtree sizes, distance sums, and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This uses the rerooting formula from L1 as the core update step. Common mistakes: Forgetting tree is undirected (need to avoid revisiting parent), incorrect initial distSum calculation, mishandling the rerooting formula signs. Real code pattern: def sumOfDistancesInTree(n, edges): build graph, subtree = [0]*n, ans = [0]*n; def dfs1(node, parent): subtree[node]=1; for nei in graph[node]: if nei!=parent: dfs1(nei,node); subtree[node]+=subtree[nei]; ans[0]+=subtree[nei]; def dfs2(node, parent): for nei in graph[node]: if nei!=parent: ans[nei]=ans[node]+n-2*subtree[nei]; dfs2(nei,node); dfs1(0,-1); dfs2(0,-1); return ans.

examples.trace: Tree: 0 connected to 1,2; 2 connected to 3,4,5. Step1: DFS1 from root 0. subtree[5]=1, subtree[4]=1, subtree[3]=1, subtree[2]=1+1+1+1=4, subtree[1]=1, subtree[0]=1+4+1=6. ans[0]= subtree[1]+subtree[2]=1+4=5? Wait careful: ans[0] is sum of distances from 0 to all others. Actually in dfs1 we compute ans[0] as sum of subtree sizes of all children because each edge contributes subtree size to distance. From 0 to 1: edge contributes subtree[1]=1. From 0 to 2: edge contributes subtree[2]=4. So ans[0]=1+4=5. But distances: from 0 to 1:1, to 2:1, to 3:2, to 4:2, to 5:2, sum=1+1+2+2+2=8. So ans[0] should be 8. Correction: In dfs1, when we go from parent to child, we add subtree[child] to ans[root] for that edge, but we also need to account for deeper edges. Actually algorithm: In dfs1, for each child, we recursively compute, then ans[root] += ans[child] + subtree[child]. Because distance sum for root includes: for each child, the sum of distances from child to nodes in its subtree (ans[child]) plus each of those nodes is one edge farther from root (so add subtree[child]). Let's recompute: dfs1(5,2): subtree[5]=1, ans[5]=0. dfs1(4,2): subtree[4]=1, ans[4]=0. dfs1(3,2): subtree[3]=1, ans[3]=0. dfs1(2,0): subtree[2]=1+1+1+1=4, ans[2]= for child 3: ans[3]+subtree[3]=0+1=1, child4:0+1=1, child5:0+1=1, total ans[2]=3. dfs1(1,0): subtree[1]=1, ans[1]=0. dfs1(0,-1): subtree[0]=6, ans[0]= for child1: ans[1]+subtree[1]=0+1=1, child2: ans[2]+subtree[2]=3+4=7, total ans[0]=8. Step2: DFS2 rerooting. ans[0]=8. Move to child1: ans[1]=ans[0]+n-2*subtree[1]=8+6-2*1=12. Move to child2: ans[2]=8+6-2*4=8+6-8=6. From 2 to child3: ans[3]=ans[2]+6-2*subtree[3]=6+6-2*1=10. Similarly ans[4]=10, ans[5]=10. Final ans=[8,12,6,10,10,10].
Single node tree. Step1: DFS1 from root 0: subtree[0]=1, ans[0]=0 (no children). Step2: DFS2: no children to reroot to. Return [0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rerooting Formula Application
ladders.desc: Given a parent's distance sum and subtree sizes, compute child's distance sum when rerooting.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core rerooting formula that allows O(n) computation of all distance sums. When we have computed the distance sum for a node u (distSum[u]) and we want to compute for its child v, we use: distSum[v] = distSum[u] + n - 2*subtreeSize[v]. The intuition: When we move the root from u to v, all nodes in v's subtree (size subtreeSize[v]) become one edge closer to the new root v, so their total distance decreases by subtreeSize[v]. Meanwhile, all nodes not in v's subtree (size n - subtreeSize[v]) become one edge farther, so their total distance increases by n - subtreeSize[v]. The net change is: -subtreeSize[v] + (n - subtreeSize[v]) = n - 2*subtreeSize[v]. Therefore, distSum[v] = distSum[u] + (n - 2*subtreeSize[v]). This formula relies on having correct subtree sizes from the first DFS. The algorithm for this sub-routine: Input: parent u, child v, n, subtreeSize array, distSum array. Output: Update distSum[v] using distSum[u]. Steps: 1) Verify v is child of u. 2) Compute distSum[v] = distSum[u] + n - 2*subtreeSize[v]. 3) Return or store distSum[v]. Time: O(1) per edge. Space: O(1) additional. Connection to L0: This formula is applied during the second DFS in the full solution to compute all distance sums efficiently. Connection to L2: This formula derives from the core insight about distance changes during rerooting. Common mistakes: Incorrect sign (should be + n - 2*subtree, not - n + 2*subtree), using wrong subtree size (must be size of v's subtree, not u's), forgetting n is total nodes. Real code pattern: def reroot_formula(parent_dist, subtree_child, n): return parent_dist + n - 2*subtree_child.

examples.trace: Apply formula: dist_child = 8 + 6 - 2*4 = 8 + 6 - 8 = 6. Explanation: When moving root from parent to child, nodes in child's subtree (size 4) get closer by 1 each → total distance decreases by 4. Nodes outside (6-4=2) get farther by 1 each → total distance increases by 2. Net change: -4+2=-2, so new sum = 8-2=6. Formula gives same: 8+6-8=6.
dist_child = 5 + 3 - 2*1 = 5+3-2=6. Change: nodes in subtree (1 node) closer by 1 → -1, nodes outside (2 nodes) farther by 1 → +2, net +1, so 5+1=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Distance Change During Rerooting
ladders.desc: Understand how the sum of distances changes when moving root from a parent to its child.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the mathematical insight that enables the O(n) solution. Consider an undirected tree with n nodes. Let u be a node, v be its child. Suppose we know distSum[u] (sum of distances from u to all nodes). When we reroot the tree at v (make v the new root), how does distSum change? The key observation: The tree can be partitioned into two sets relative to the edge (u,v): 1) The subtree rooted at v (including v and all its descendants), size S = subtreeSize[v]. 2) The rest of the tree (all other nodes), size n - S. When we move root from u to v: Every node in v's subtree becomes one edge closer to the new root v (since previously distance from u to those nodes went through edge u-v, now v is root so that edge is removed). So total distance decreases by S (each of S nodes gets 1 unit closer). Every node not in v's subtree becomes one edge farther (since previously distance from u to those nodes didn't use edge u-v, but now from v we must traverse u-v to reach them). So total distance increases by (n - S). Therefore, net change = (n - S) - S = n - 2S. Thus, distSum[v] = distSum[u] + n - 2S. This formula is powerful because it allows computing all distance sums from one known distSum in O(1) per edge. The intuition relies on tree properties: no cycles, unique paths. The algorithm to apply this: For each edge (u,v), if we know distSum[u] and S, we can compute distSum[v]. We need S from first DFS. Time to understand this logic: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic directly gives the rerooting formula used in L1. Connection to L3: This builds on understanding subtree sizes from L3. Common mistakes: Confusing which nodes get closer/farther, forgetting the tree is undirected, miscalculating S. Real code pattern: Conceptual, no code needed, just the formula derivation.

examples.input: Tree with n=6, edge u-v where subtreeSize[v]=4, distSum[u]=8
examples.trace: Partition: v's subtree size S=4, other nodes count=6-4=2. Change: nodes in subtree (4 nodes) get closer by 1 each → total decrease 4. Other nodes (2 nodes) get farther by 1 each → total increase 2. Net change = 2-4 = -2. So distSum[v] = 8 + (-2) = 6. Using formula: 8 + 6 - 2*4 = 6.
S=1, other nodes=2. Change: 1 node closer by 1 → -1, 2 nodes farther by 1 → +2, net +1. distSum[v]=5+1=6. Formula: 5+3-2*1=6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Subtree Sizes via DFS
ladders.desc: Perform a DFS to compute the size of each node's subtree in a tree.
ladders.explanation: Subtree size is fundamental for the rerooting formula. For a tree rooted at a node, the subtree size of a node is the number of nodes in the subtree rooted at that node (including itself). This can be computed efficiently with a single DFS traversal. The intuition: In a tree, each node's subtree size equals 1 (for itself) plus the sum of subtree sizes of all its children. This is a recursive definition that lends itself to post-order DFS: we compute children's sizes first, then aggregate. The algorithm: Input: tree as adjacency list, root node. Output: array subtreeSize where subtreeSize[i] = size of subtree rooted at i. Steps: 1) Initialize subtreeSize array with zeros. 2) Define DFS function dfs(node, parent): a) Set subtreeSize[node] = 1 (count itself). b) For each neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor != parent (to avoid going back to parent in undirected tree), recursively call dfs(neighbor, node), then add subtreeSize[neighbor] to subtreeSize[node]. c) Return subtreeSize[node]. 3) Call dfs(root, -1). Time: O(n) since each edge visited once. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and array. Connection to L2: Subtree sizes are needed to compute the S in the rerooting formula. Connection to L4: This uses DFS traversal from L4. Common mistakes: Forgetting to avoid parent (causing infinite recursion), not initializing size to 1, incorrect aggregation order. Real code pattern: def compute_subtree_sizes(graph, root): n=len(graph); subtree=[0]*n; def dfs(node,parent): subtree[node]=1; for nei in graph[node]: if nei!=parent: dfs(nei,node); subtree[node]+=subtree[nei]; dfs(root,-1); return subtree.

examples.trace: DFS from root 0: Visit 0, size=1. Neighbor 1: dfs(1,0): size[1]=1 (no children), return 1. Add to size[0]: now 2. Neighbor 2: dfs(2,0): size[2]=1. Neighbor 3: dfs(3,2): size[3]=1, return, add to size[2]=2. Neighbor 4: dfs(4,2): size[4]=1, add to size[2]=3. Neighbor 5: dfs(5,2): size[5]=1, add to size[2]=4. Return 4 to parent 0. Add to size[0]: 2+4=6. Final sizes: [6,1,4,1,1,1].
DFS from 0: size[0]=1. Neighbor 1: dfs(1,0): size[1]=1. Neighbor 2: dfs(2,1): size[2]=1, return, add to size[1]=2. Return 2 to 0, add to size[0]=3. Sizes: [3,2,1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — DFS Traversal on a Tree
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search on a tree structure, avoiding cycles by tracking parent.
ladders.explanation: DFS traversal is essential for processing trees. A tree is an undirected acyclic connected graph. DFS visits nodes depth-first, exploring as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In trees, we must avoid revisiting nodes; since there are no cycles, we simply avoid going back to the parent. The intuition: Start at a root, recursively visit all children (neighbors except parent), ensuring each node is visited exactly once. This is O(n) time. The algorithm: Input: adjacency list representation of tree, starting node root. Output: Traversal order or perform some operation at each node. Steps: 1) Define visited set or use parent tracking. 2) Define function dfs(node, parent): a) Process node (e.g., print or compute something). b) For each neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor != parent, recursively call dfs(neighbor, node). 3) Call dfs(root, -1). Time: O(n) where n is number of nodes, since each edge visited twice (once from each end) but recursion ensures O(n). Space: O(n) for recursion stack in worst-case (line tree). Connection to L3: DFS is used to compute subtree sizes. Connection to L5: This builds on graph traversal concepts. Common mistakes: Not handling undirected edges (need parent parameter), using visited set without care (trees don't need visited if using parent, but could use it), infinite recursion if parent not tracked. Real code pattern: def dfs_tree(graph, root): def dfs(node, parent): for nei in graph[node]: if nei != parent: dfs(nei, node); dfs(root, -1).

examples.output: Traversal order: 0,1,2,3 (or similar depending on neighbor order)
examples.trace: Start at root 0. Process 0. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Suppose visit 1 first: dfs(1,0): process 1, neighbors of 1: only 0 which is parent, so return. Back to 0, next neighbor 2: dfs(2,0): process 2, neighbors: 0 (parent) and 3. Visit 3: dfs(3,2): process 3, neighbors only 2 (parent), return. Done. Order: 0,1,2,3.
Start 0: process 0. Neighbor 1: dfs(1,0): process 1. Neighbors of 1: 0 (parent) and 2. Visit 2: dfs(2,1): process 2, neighbors only 1 (parent), return. Order: 0,1,2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Tree as a Graph
ladders.desc: Understand basic tree properties: undirected, acyclic, connected, with n nodes and n-1 edges.
ladders.explanation: A tree is a fundamental graph structure with specific properties that make algorithms like rerooting DP possible. Key properties: 1) Undirected: edges have no direction. 2) Acyclic: contains no cycles; there is exactly one simple path between any two nodes. 3) Connected: all nodes are reachable from each other. 4) For n nodes, there are exactly n-1 edges. These properties imply that removing any edge disconnects the tree, and adding any edge creates a cycle. The intuition for rerooting: Because trees are acyclic, when we move the root, the distance change can be computed locally using the edge between parent and child. The tree can be rooted at any node, and subtree sizes are well-defined. This level is about recognizing these properties. No complex algorithm, just definitions. Connection to L4: Trees are traversed using DFS/BFS. Common mistakes: Confusing trees with general graphs (trees have no cycles), misunderstanding connectivity. Real code pattern: Represent tree as adjacency list: graph = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u).

examples.output: Valid tree: 4 nodes, 3 edges, connected, no cycles.
examples.trace: Check: number edges = 3 = 4-1, so satisfies edge count. Check connectivity: from node 0, can reach 1,2,3 via paths 0-1, 0-1-2, 0-1-2-3, so connected. Check cycles: no edges form a cycle (e.g., no edge between 0 and 3). So it's a tree.
Edges count = 3 = 3-1? Actually 3 edges for 3 nodes, but 3-1=2, so edge count mismatch already indicates not a tree. Also, there is a cycle 0-1-2-0, so not acyclic. Thus not a tree.
